docs.title: Asset Forge
docs.text:
Build material assets from texture folders, mark object assets, render custom previews, and dry run everything first, all from the Asset Browser sidebar.

Asset Forge is a Blender 5.1 add-on that simplifies and automates common asset-related tasks directly inside the Asset Browser.

Superhive page not up yet Documentation

Material Assets: Build PBR material assets from texture folders with customizable naming keywords, UDIM detection, overwrite policies, and manual overrides. Object Assets: Mark Selected Objects, Selected Collection, or All with automatic catalog writing and hierarchy placement controls. Preview Renders: Render custom thumbnails with configurable camera, lighting, thumbnail size, and selection scope. Dry Run & Reports: Preview changes and review expandable reports.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Requirement Details Blender 5.1 or newer Saved file A saved .blend file is needed for object asset catalogs and rendered preview output

Support for earlier Blender versions is planned for a future update.

Download the release .zip. In Blender, open Edit > Preferences. In Get Extensions or Add-ons, open the menu and click Install from Disk..., select the zip, and enable Asset Forge. The add-on appears in the Asset Browser sidebar under the Asset Forge tab.

docs.title: Updates
docs.text:
Asset Forge is actively developed with more features and improvements planned.

Current public addon version: 1.0.0
Found a bug or have a feature suggestion? Reach out through the Superhive inbox or join the Discord server. Feedback helps shape future updates.

docs.title: License
docs.text: Asset Forge is licensed under GPL-3.0-or-later.

docs.title: v1.0.0
docs.text: Initial release.

Initial release features: material asset building from texture folders, manual overrides, customizable naming keywords, UDIM detection, Skip Existing / Update Generated Only / Force Replace Existing overwrite policies, object assets for Selected Objects / Selected Collection / All, hierarchy controls, preview renders, Quick and Eevee modes, clear asset previews, dry run, and reports.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
Does it require a saved .blend file?

Yes — Blender needs a saved file to write asset catalogs and output rendered previews. Material builds work without a saved file, but object assets and preview renders require one.

What Blender versions are supported?

Asset Forge requires Blender 5.1 or newer. Support for earlier versions is planned for a future update.

Can I preview changes before applying?

Yes. Enable Dry Run before any action — material builds, object marking, or preview renders — to see exactly what will happen without changing anything.

docs.title: Material Assets
docs.text:
Can it work without normal maps?

Yes. Required maps are inferred from what's detected in your texture root — only base color is always required. If your folders don't contain normal maps, they won't be required.

Does it support UDIMs?

Yes. Enable Detect UDIM Tiles in Build Options. Tile sets numbered 1001, 1002, 1003 etc. are grouped automatically.

Will it overwrite my existing materials?

Only if the selected overwrite policy allows it. Update Generated Only (the default) protects user-made materials. Skip Existing never overwrites. Use Dry Run to check what would be affected before running a build.

Does it build complex shader graphs?

Not yet — it creates clean Principled BSDF or Diffuse setups from detected maps.

Can I use custom node groups instead of Principled BSDF?

Not currently. The add-on builds either Principled BSDF or Diffuse material graphs.

My textures aren't being detected. What should I check?

Open Texture Naming Keywords and verify the keywords match your filename conventions. Also check that your textures are inside the Texture Root folder (or its subfolders) and that Folder Tails includes any intermediate folder names like maps or textures.

docs.title: Object Assets
docs.text:
Where are asset catalogs saved?

In Blender's asset catalog system, which is stored alongside the .blend file. The file must be saved before catalogs can be written.

What do the hierarchy placement options do?

They control where parent objects, child objects, and assembly collections are placed in the catalog tree. Options include same catalog as the collection, a dedicated subcatalog, or a top-level catalog path. Use Dry Run to preview the structure.

docs.title: Preview Renders
docs.text:
Does it work with Cycles?

Preview renders use either Quick (Workbench-based) or Eevee. Cycles is not currently supported as a preview render engine.

Can I cancel a render in progress?

Yes — Eevee renders support cancel with Esc. Quick renders are fast enough that cancellation isn't usually needed.

Why do Asset Browser selections clear after rendering?

This is a known Blender limitation. The selection state in the Asset Browser may reset after a preview render completes. Your assets and previews are not affected.

docs.title: Other
docs.text:
Does it pack textures into the .blend?

Not currently. Textures remain as external file references.

Can I undo a material build?

Yes — use Ctrl+Z to undo. Alternatively, re-run the build with a different overwrite policy.

Where can I report bugs or suggest features?

Reach out through the Superhive inbox or join the Discord server. Feedback helps shape future updates.

docs.title: Dry Run & Reports
docs.text: Every action in Asset Forge — material builds, object asset marking, and preview renders — supports dry run. Preview exactly what will happen before anything changes, and review detailed results in the report panel.

docs.title: Enabling Dry Run
docs.text:
The Dry Run toggle is in the report section at the top of the Asset Forge panel. Enable it before running any action.

When dry run is enabled:

  • Material builds show what materials would be created, skipped, or overwritten — without actually creating or modifying anything.
  • Object asset marking shows what objects would be marked and where they'd be placed in the catalog — without writing any asset data.
  • Preview renders show which assets would be rendered — without producing any images.

After every action, results appear in the report section at the top of the panel. Summary lines show action counts, Selected Report shows per-item details, and Issues lists problems. The prefix changes between "Dry run -" and "Done -" so you can tell whether changes were simulated or applied.

docs.title: When to Use Dry Run
docs.text:
• First time with a new texture set — See what the scanner finds before building materials.
  • Before overwriting — Check exactly which existing materials would be replaced.
  • Testing hierarchy controls — Preview the catalog structure before committing object assets.
  • Large batch renders — Verify the correct assets are selected before starting a long render.
  • After changing keywords or overrides — Confirm the new settings detect what you expect.

Download the release .zip from Superhive. In Blender, open Edit > Preferences. In Get Extensions or Add-ons, open the menu and click Install from Disk..., select the downloaded zip, and enable Asset Forge. The add-on appears in the Asset Browser sidebar under the Asset Forge tab.

docs.title: Where It Appears
docs.text:
Asset Forge lives entirely in the Asset Browser sidebar. Open any Asset Browser area and look for the Asset Forge tab on the right side.

The panel is organized into sections:

  • Reports — Dry run toggle and action results (top)
  • Material Assets — Texture sources, build options, Make Material Assets
  • Object Assets — Scope, hierarchy controls, Make Object Assets
  • Preview Setup — Environment, camera, setup preview
  • Render Preview — Quick and Eevee rendering with batch controls

docs.title: Building Material Assets
docs.text:
1. Open the Asset Browser and expand the Asset Forge panel.
  2. In Texture Sources, set your Texture Root folder — or enable manual folder overrides for specific map types.
  3. Expand Texture Naming Keywords if your textures use non-standard naming conventions.
  4. In Build Options, choose a Shader (Principled BSDF or Diffuse) and an Overwrite Policy.
  5. Click Make Material Assets.

Tip

Enable Dry Run first to preview what will be created, skipped, or overwritten — without changing anything.
Save the .blend file. In Object Assets, choose Selected Objects, Selected Collection, or All, adjust Parents, Children, and Assembly placement, then run Make Object Assets.

In Preview Setup, configure environment and camera settings, then use Refresh Setup Preview to inspect the rig. In Render Preview, choose Quick or Eevee, decide whether Selected reads from Outliner / Viewport or Asset Browser, then pick Selected, Collection/Catalogue, or All. Setup preview is static and needs an open 3D Viewport. Refresh it after lighting, camera, preview mesh, or background changes.

Save your .blend file first. Start with Dry Run on your first material build. Check the report panel after every action and expand Selected Report or Issues for details. Most texture packs work with the default PBR naming keywords.

docs.title: Material Assets
docs.text: Asset Forge builds PBR material assets from texture folders. Point it at a root directory, and it scans for texture maps, wires up shader graphs, and registers everything as assets in the Asset Browser.

docs.title: Texture Root
docs.text:
Set Texture Root to a folder containing your texture sets. The scanner traverses subfolders recursively and groups textures by their base name, looking for common PBR map suffixes.

A typical folder structure might look like:

textures/
├── brick_wall/
│   ├── brick_wall_basecolor.png
│   ├── brick_wall_normal.png
│   ├── brick_wall_roughness.png
│   └── brick_wall_height.png
├── metal_plate/
│   ├── metal_plate_basecolor.png
│   ├── metal_plate_normal.png
│   ├── metal_plate_metallic.png
│   └── metal_plate_roughness.png

Each subfolder with a valid base color map becomes one material asset.

docs.title: Manual Overrides
docs.text:
Enable per-map folder overrides to point any map type at a different directory. This is useful when your maps are organized by type rather than by material:

base_color/
├── brick_wall.png
├── metal_plate.png
normal/
├── brick_wall.png
├── metal_plate.png

When an override is enabled for a map type, it replaces the corresponding result from the Texture Root scan for that map type.

docs.title: Combine Root + Overrides
docs.text:
When enabled, required map types are combined from both the root scan and the enabled overrides. When disabled, overrides fully replace the root scan results for their map types.

Example

With Combine Root + Overrides enabled: if your root folder contains base color and normal maps, and you enable a roughness override pointing to a separate folder, all three map types become required.

docs.title: Texture Naming Keywords
docs.text:
The scanner matches textures by keywords in the filename or parent folder name. Default keywords cover common conventions like basecolor, diffuse, albedo for base color, normal, nrm for normals, etc.

You can customize keywords for each map type:

  • Base Color — basecolor, base_color, base-colour, base_colour, basecolour, albedo, diffuse, color, colour, col
  • Normal — normalgl, normal_gl, normaldx, normal_dx, normal, nrm, nor
  • Roughness — roughness, rough, rgh
  • Metallic — metalness, metallic, metal
  • Height — displacement, height, bump, disp
  • Alpha — opacity, alpha, mask

Folder Tails are shared keywords for parent folder names like maps and textures — folders matching these tails are traversed into rather than treated as material names.

Principled BSDF builds a full PBR material using base color, roughness, metallic, normal, height, and alpha when detected. Diffuse builds a simpler graph that still uses base color plus normal and height detail when available.

docs.title: Overwrite Policy
docs.text:
Controls what happens when a material with the same name already exists:

  • Update Generated Only — Only overwrite materials that were previously created by Asset Forge. User-made materials are protected. (default)
  • Skip Existing — Never overwrite. Existing materials are left untouched.
  • Force Replace Existing — Replace any existing material with the same name.

Warning

Force Replace Existing will replace hand-made materials. Use Dry Run first to check what will be affected.
When enabled, the scanner detects numbered tiles like 1001, 1002, and 1003, groups them as one UDIM material, and treats the set as a tiled texture input in the generated material.

docs.title: Requirements Inference
docs.text:
Required maps are inferred automatically from what the scanner detects in your texture root. If every texture set in your root folder includes a normal map, normal maps become required — and any set missing a normal map will be skipped.

Base color is always required regardless of what's detected.

Tip

Check the report panel after a build (or dry run) to see exactly which sets were skipped and why.

Configure texture sources and build options, optionally enable Dry Run, click Make Material Assets, and review the report. Per-material details include required maps, missing maps, detected UDIM tiles, and source texture paths.

docs.title: Object Assets
docs.text: Asset Forge marks objects as Blender assets and writes their catalog entries automatically. Select what you want to mark, choose how the catalog should be organized, and run it in one click.
Choose what to mark as assets: Selected Objects, Selected Collection, or All.

Asset catalogs are written from the collection hierarchy. Objects use the deepest matching collection path. If no scene collection path can be resolved, Asset Forge falls back to Uncategorized. The .blend file must be saved before object assets are created.

docs.title: Hierarchy Controls
docs.text: When your scene has parent-child object relationships, you can control how they're organized in the asset catalog:

docs.title: Parents
docs.text:
Controls where parent objects (objects that have children) are placed:

  • Disabled — Do not create separate assets for parent objects.
  • Same Catalog — Place parent assets in the same catalog as their collection.
  • Subcatalog — Place parent assets in a Parents subcatalog under their collection catalog.
  • Top-Level — Place parent assets in a top-level Parents catalog path.
Children controls where child object assets are placed: Disabled, Same Catalog, Subcatalog, or Top-Level.

Assembly controls whether generated assembly collection assets are created and where they are placed: Disabled, Same Catalog, Subcatalog, or Top-Level. When enabled for a parent object, Asset Forge creates or reuses a <root>_complete collection containing the root object and its recursive children, then marks that collection as an asset.

Save the .blend file, choose Selected Objects, Selected Collection, or All, adjust Parents, Children, and Assembly placement, click Make Object Assets, and review the report. Parent objects can produce both an object asset and an assembly collection asset if Assembly is enabled.

Asset Forge renders custom asset preview thumbnails with control over camera, lighting, thumbnail size, and selection scope. Rendered preview jobs require a saved .blend file and at least one open 3D Viewport. Output images are written to an mma_thumbs folder next to the saved file before they are assigned back to asset previews.

Lighting types: HDRI uses an environment image from World Images. Sun uses directional lighting with strength, softness, rotation, and elevation. Area uses a softer studio-style light with configurable distance and size. Point uses an omni-directional light with configurable distance and shadow radius.
HDRI mode uses the selected environment image plus World Rotation. Sun, Area, and Point lighting use a solid Background Color.

docs.title: Transparent Background
docs.text: Enable transparent background for setup previews when you want the object rendered without a visible world behind it.
Perspective uses focal length for a more photographic look. Orthographic removes perspective distortion for flatter catalog-style thumbnails.
Edge Padding (%) controls framing. Positive values add more border. Negative values tighten the frame and can crop the object.
Camera Preset includes Isometric, Front, Side, Top, and 3/4. Fine-tune with Camera Rotation, Focal Length, and clip distances.

Setup Preview renders a static sample scene so you can inspect the current camera and lighting rig. Choose a Preview Mesh such as Monkey, Cube, UV Sphere, Icosphere, Cylinder, Cone, or Torus, optionally Include Floor, and toggle Transparent Background. Refresh it after lighting, camera, preview mesh, or background changes.

docs.title: Quick
docs.text: A fast Workbench-based render path. Produces approximate previews in minimal time. Best for bulk operations where speed matters more than visual fidelity.

docs.title: Eevee
docs.text:
A full Eevee render using the add-on's render rig. Produces higher quality previews with proper lighting, shadows, and materials.

  • Supports cancel with Esc during rendering.
  • Takes longer than Quick, especially for large batches.

When Selected is active, targets can come from Outliner / Viewport or Asset Browser. Quick Preview expects asset-marked selected objects when using Outliner / Viewport. Eevee can render the current selection directly before the preview is attached to the datablock. Asset Browser uses the current local asset selection.
Render previews for all assets in the active asset catalogue. If Asset Forge cannot resolve a current catalogue selection, it falls back to the active collection.
Render previews for every asset-marked object and collection in the current file.
Removes custom preview images from the currently selected local assets in the Asset Browser, reverting them to Blender's default auto-generated previews.

docs.title: Texture Detection
docs.text: Asset Forge's texture scanner automatically identifies and groups PBR texture maps from your folders. This page explains how the detection works and how to customize it.

docs.title: How Scanning Works
docs.text:
When you set a Texture Root and run Make Material Assets, the scanner:

  1. Traverses the root folder and its subfolders recursively.
  2. Identifies image files by their extension.
  3. Matches each image to a map type based on keywords in the filename or parent folder name.
  4. Groups images by their base name (the part before the map type suffix).
  5. Creates one material per group, connecting each map to the appropriate shader input.
Supported map types include Base Color, Normal, Roughness, Metallic, Height, and Alpha. Height maps are used as bump and height detail rather than true displacement.

docs.title: Naming Keywords
docs.text:
The scanner matches map types by looking for keywords in the filename. These keywords are fully customizable in the Texture Naming Keywords section of the panel.

Each map type has its own keyword list. Keywords are matched case-insensitively against the filename (without extension).

docs.title: Folder Tails
docs.text:
Folder tail keywords identify parent folders that should be traversed into rather than treated as material names. Common examples: maps, textures, tex.

If your texture folders are structured like:

brick_wall/
├── maps/
│   ├── basecolor.png
│   ├── normal.png
│   └── roughness.png

The maps folder tail ensures the scanner looks inside maps/ and attributes the textures to brick_wall.

docs.title: Customizing Keywords
docs.text:
To add or modify keywords:

  1. Expand Texture Naming Keywords in the Material Assets section.
  2. Edit the keyword field for the map type you want to change.
  3. Keywords are comma-separated.

Tip

Use Dry Run after changing keywords to verify the scanner detects your textures correctly.
Detect UDIM Tiles groups numbered tile suffixes like 1001, 1002, and 1003 into one material and uses a <UDIM> token path behind the scenes while keeping the tile list together under one asset.

docs.title: Requirements Inference
docs.text:
Required maps are determined automatically:

  1. The scanner examines what map types exist across all texture sets in your root folder.
  2. If a map type appears consistently, it becomes required.
  3. Any texture set missing a required map is skipped.
  4. Base color is always required — a set without a base color map is never valid.

This means the requirements adapt to your texture library automatically. If all your sets include normal maps, normal maps become required. If only some do, they remain optional.

docs.title: Combine Root + Overrides
docs.text:
When Combine Root + Overrides is enabled, required map types are merged from both the root scan and the enabled manual overrides. When disabled, overrides fully replace the root scan results for their map types.

Example

Combine enabled: Root detects base color + normal. Override adds roughness. All three become required.

Combine disabled: Root detects base color + normal. Override adds roughness. Only base color + roughness are required (root normals are replaced by the override scope).

Save the .blend file first. If HDRI lighting is enabled, make sure the selected environment image still exists in the World Images folder. Preview rendering also requires at least one open 3D Viewport. Custom rendered previews require the target objects or collections to already be marked as assets.

docs.title: Materials are skipped
docs.text:
• Missing base color. Every texture set needs at least a base color map. Check that your base color files match the expected naming keywords.
  • Requirements inference. The scanner infers required maps from what it detects across all sets. If most sets include normal maps, any set missing a normal map will be skipped. Use Dry Run to see which maps are required and which sets are being skipped.
  • Combine Root + Overrides. When enabled, this merges requirements from both sources — which can make more map types required than intended. Try disabling it if too many sets are being skipped.
  • Check the report. The report panel shows the exact skip reason for each material.

docs.title: Textures not detected
docs.text:
• Check naming conventions. The scanner matches by keywords in the filename. Open Texture Naming Keywords to see the current keywords and add yours if needed.
  • Check folder structure. Make sure your textures are inside the Texture Root folder or its subfolders.
  • Folder tail keywords. If your textures are inside a subfolder like maps/ or textures/, make sure that folder name is listed in Folder Tails.
  • File format. Verify your texture files are in a format Blender supports (PNG, JPEG, TIFF, EXR, etc.).

Open Edit > Preferences, then use Get Extensions or Add-ons to confirm Asset Forge is installed and enabled. In the Asset Browser, press N to show the sidebar if needed. Asset Forge only appears in the Asset Browser editor.

docs.title: Asset Browser selections clear after preview renders
docs.text: This is a known limitation. Blender may reset the Asset Browser selection after a preview render completes. Your assets and previews are not affected — only the visual selection state in the browser.
Save the file first. Make sure the target objects are included in the selected scope: Selected Objects, Selected Collection, or All.

docs.title: UDIM tiles not grouped
docs.text:
• Enable UDIM detection. Make sure Detect UDIM Tiles is enabled in Build Options.
  • Check numbering. UDIM tiles must use the standard 1001, 1002, 1003 numbering pattern in the filename.
  • Check separators. The tile number should be separated from the base name by a dot, underscore, or hyphen (e.g., wall_basecolor.1001.png or wall_basecolor_1001.png).

docs.title: Dry run shows unexpected results
docs.text:
• Check overwrite policy. The policy affects what the dry run reports as "would be overwritten" vs "would be skipped."
  • Check Combine Root + Overrides. This setting changes which maps are required, which affects what gets skipped.
  • Review keywords. If textures aren't being detected, the naming keywords may not match your files.

Tip

When reporting a bug, include the full report panel output — expand both Selected Report and Issues and screenshot or copy the text. This helps diagnose the problem much faster.

docs.title: Thumbnail Size
docs.text: Use Thumbnail Size to pick a square preview resolution. Presets run from 32p through 1024p, and Custom lets you enter your own pixel size.
